title: Daily Routine
description: Basic phrasal verbs to talk about your everyday life

points.definition: To leave your bed after sleeping

examples.english: I get up at 7 every morning.
What time do you get up on weekends?

points.notes: Very common in daily conversations. No object needed.

examples.english: The baby always wakes up at night.

points.notes: Difference from get up: wake up = open your eyes, get up = leave the bed.

points.definition: To put yourself in a horizontal position, usually to rest

examples.english: I need to lie down for a while.
The doctor told me to lie down and relax.

points.notes: Often used when tired or sick.

points.definition: To stop working or doing something for a short time

examples.english: Let's have a break and get some coffee.
We've been studying for two hours. Time to have a break!

points.notes: British English. In US English, 'take a break' is more common.

points.title: Practice: Daily Routine
points.description: Complete the exercises with get up, wake up, lie down, and have a break

questions.sentence: I'm very tired. I want to ______ ______ (stop sleeping) late tomorrow.
questions.feedback: Correct! 'Wake up' means to stop sleeping.

questions.sentence: Every morning, I get ______ at 7 o'clock.
questions.feedback: Good! 'Get up' means to leave your bed after sleeping.

questions.context: A: You look tired. B: Yes, I need to ______ ______ for 10 minutes.
questions.feedback: Great! 'Lie down' is perfect when you need to rest.

questions.question: What's the difference between 'wake up' and 'get up'?
options: They mean exactly the same thing
Wake up = stop sleeping, get up = leave the bed
Wake up = leave the bed, get up = stop sleeping
Wake up is for objects, get up is for people
questions.feedback: Exactly! Wake up = open your eyes, get up = leave the bed.

questions.instruction: Now replace 'the TV' with the pronoun 'it'.
questions.feedback: Important! With pronouns, separable phrasal verbs MUST separate: 'Turn it off' (not 'turn off it').
